title: Brønsted-Lowry theory of acids and bases
summary: 9701 AS acids and bases — Brønsted-Lowry definitions, conjugate pairs, pH, and strong vs weak acids. PhET Acid-Base Solutions integrated.
learningObjectives: Define Brønsted-Lowry acids and bases and identify conjugate pairs.
Distinguish strong and weak acids in terms of degree of ionisation.
Calculate pH of strong monoprotic acids and strong bases.
Write Ka expressions and understand the link between Ka and pKa.

simpleExplanation.title: Proton donors and acceptors
simpleExplanation.summary: Brønsted-Lowry: acids donate H⁺, bases accept H⁺. Every acid has a conjugate base (minus one H⁺); every base has a conjugate acid (plus one H⁺).
steps: Identify the acid and base in an equilibrium — e.g. CH₃COOH + H₂O ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H₃O⁺.
Label conjugate pairs: acid/conjugate base differ by one H⁺.
Strong acids ionise fully — [H⁺] equals initial acid concentration (for monoprotic).
Weak acids establish equilibrium — use Ka and an ICE table for pH calculations.

flashcards.back: pKa = −log Ka — smaller pKa means stronger acid.

sections.content: Acid-base chemistry runs through **9701 AS and A Level** — titration curves, buffer systems, and equilibrium constants all build on **Brønsted-Lowry** definitions from topic 7.2.
pH = −log[H⁺] | pOH = −log[OH⁻] | pH + pOH = 14 at 25 °C | Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA]

sections.question: Identify the conjugate base of HSO₄⁻ in water and write the equation.
sections.solution:
HSO₄⁻ donates H⁺ to water:

HSO₄⁻ + H₂O ⇌ SO₄²⁻ + H₃O⁺

Conjugate base of HSO₄⁻ is **SO₄²⁻**.

sections.content: In conjugate pair questions, **never** include H₂O as part of the pair — the pair is the two species differing by one H⁺ on the same side of the equation (or across the equilibrium excluding the solvent).

sections.label: Mark an acids and bases question
